text.title: Consistency and Standards Examples
text.summary: Real-world consistency examples, anti-patterns, and agentic audit workflow for terminology and token enforcement.

example.note: iOS: back button always in top-left, swipe from edge to go back, pull to refresh, tab bar at bottom. Platform conventions held without exception across every iOS app from Apple.

Google products: consistent Material Design across Search, Gmail, Drive, Docs. Same account menu, same search pattern, shared iconography. Users transfer learned behavior between products at zero learning cost.
Page 1: [Save]. Page 2: [Submit]. Page 3: [Done]. Same action, three different labels. User re-evaluates button meaning at every screen instead of acting automatically.
Page 1: [Cancel] [Save]. Page 2: [Save] [Cancel]. Button order reversed. User trained by page 1 clicks Save when intending Cancel — destructive error caused by inconsistency.

section.title: Agentic Workflow — Terminology Audit
section.p: Agent reads all UI copy from i18n files, component templates, or scraped live pages. Groups synonym clusters for each UX concept. Produces terminology audit report with recommended canonical term per concept. Output feeds into design system glossary and component documentation update.
section.reference: Audit template for visual, functional, terminology, and platform compliance
LLM prompt for generating a terminology audit from a list of button labels

section.title: Agentic Workflow — Token Enforcement
section.p: Agent scans codebase for hardcoded CSS color and spacing values that bypass design tokens. Generates migration list with file:line references and recommended token replacements. Can be run in CI via ripgrep or stylelint rules.
section.reference: Bash script for scanning a codebase for hardcoded hex colors outside design tokens

antipattern.description: Agent proposes canonical terms without platform HIG validation.
antipattern.reason: LLMs pick the most common term in existing usage, which may be a legacy wrong term. Always validate canonical term selection against Apple HIG, Material Design, or Windows Fluent before adopting.

antipattern.description: Agent treats locale-specific idiomatic differences as inconsistencies (Submit in en-US, Send in en-GB).
antipattern.reason: Scope terminology audits to a single locale first. Cross-locale differences may be intentional and correct.
